1998 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival

[Image: Graphic of a stylized snake depicted in Pacific Northwest Native American art style, slithering right to left.]
U.S. Classic Championships • U.S. Relay Championships
Cle Elum, Washington

The web site for the Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival, featuring the U.S. Classic Champs & U.S. Relay Champs, is now up and running. The Festival, hosted by Cascade, will be held in Cle Elum on August 7, 8, and 9, 1998. Thank you to Howard Halter, for your fine work on developing the site that can be found at: "http://weber.u.washington.edu/~bill3/usch98"

Rick Breseman, Meet Director

T-Shirts for now and this summer

If you are looking for the perfect gift for yourself or a friend that is practical yet attractive (includes the above logo designed by Debbie Newell!), look no farther; you can purchase a 1998 US Champs T-shirt. (Quantities are limited until we do our major printing in early summer, so don't miss out: $12 for short-sleeve. If you want to become of the meet staff, just contact Rick Breseman the meet director, and then you can purchase the for-staff-only shirt at $10 for short-sleeve or $14 for long-sleeve.)

Upcoming Events

[Image: Graphic of a dark triangle in a square box]

SKI-O

We are hoping for a ski-o meet this year. Bruce McAlister and Eric Bone revised the Lake Wenatchee map this past summer. There is limited vegetation information, and, of course, the ski routes do not always follow marked roads or trails, but the map is a substantial improvement over the park ski maps. At present the meet is tentatively set for Saturday, February 28, the weekend after the WIOL Championships. Since snow is always a question, be sure to check the hot line. Those who wish can stop Saturday night at Bruce’s cabin at Scenic, and ski either to the hot springs or the Iron Gate trail.

1998 Nike World Masters Games Orienteering Event

So, what are you doing next summer?

The countdown continues as eight months remain until the largest multisport competition in the world, the 1998 NIKE World Masters Games. Portland, Oregon, and several surrounding communities are gearing up to welcome 25,000 athletes from over 100 countries. 400 of these athletes will descend on Portland, OR and Goldendale, WA to compete in Orienteering.

The World Masters Games are looking for orienteers to volunteer their time and vast knowledge to help the Orienteering Sports Committee plan their events. Besides gaining invaluable event organizing experience and participating in a once in a lifetime event, volunteers will walk away with lots of cool stuff.

All Orienteering Committee Volunteers will be outfitted in limited edition NIKE polo shirts, caps and waist packs and receive coupons and discounts at many area merchants. At the conclusion of the Games, volunteers will receive an Official Volunteer Certificate and regal treatment at the Volunteer Appreciation Party. Perhaps the best gift is knowing that you had a significant role in promoting and producing a world-class international Orienteering Meet in front of a world wide audience. ESPN and ABC Sports are covering the Games and will feature Orienteering on one of their segments.

So what exactly is going on? Challenging O-action will take place in Portland and Goldendale, WA, from August 10-14, 1998. There will be a Training Event as well as a Short Course and Classic 1 and Classic 2 meets. Medals will be awarded for both A and B levels in each event.

With the help of an organized and enthusiastic Orienteering community, the event will be an incredible success. For more information on positions available on the Orienteering Sports Committee, please contact Gorden Bowen, Orienteering Sport Volunteer Director, at (503) 235-6876, or by email at gordonb@europa.com. To receive a volunteer application form or more information on the 1998 NIKE World Masters Games please call (503) 226-1998, or visit the on-line Web site at http://www.worldmasters.org.

Winter O' Series

Debbie Newell, COC

This year’s Winter O’ Series began at Kelsey Creek Park in Bellevue.

For those of you unfamiliar with the O’ Series, these are the basic rules:

Seven meets have been chosen to make up the O’ Series (see below). Just run the longest course at any of these meets. Your best 4 scores will be combined to give your Series total (see scoring system below). The top 3 males and females will receive awards to be given out at the WIOL Championship meet at Marymoor Park. If you have any questions or suggestions, let me know.

Four Days of ‘A’ meets in B.C. This Summer!

The Greater Vancouver Orienteering Club (GVOC) is hosting 4 days of ‘A’ meets July 25-28, 1998. GVOC are hosting the Canadian Championships and Canadian Short Distance Championships at Logan Lake, B.C. on July 25-26, and 27, 1998. The Canadian Relay Championships will be held on Jul 28 in Kamloops, B.C. on the Grasslands map. Stay tuned for more information.

News and Notes

[Image: Graphic of a dark triangle in a square box]

Cascade Officer Elections at January Meeting

Cascade Orienteering Club will be electing its 1998 officers during the January 11 meeting at the Santa Fe Cafe in Finney Ridge (see the schedule for more information). If you are interested in helping COC by volunteering to be an officer, please contact Bill Cusworth before the meeting.

Dinner will be at 5:00 pm, followed by a presentation by Ardis Dull on her trip to the World Championships last summer. The business meeting will start at 6:15 pm and continue until 8. Please come!

Canoe on Bowron Lake

There is a possibility of a canoe trip on the Bowron Lake Circuit the week between the Canadian Champs and the North American Champs at Kamloops (July 26-August 1). This approximately 120 km canoe trip in the heart of the Canadian Rockies with glimpses of Mt Robson is one of the premier canoe trips in the world. We need a group of 7 to 12. The trip is not demanding physically, but does require prior knowledge of canoeing. Permit cost is $45 per canoe (2 persons), canoe rental (2 persons) at Bowron Lake is about $175 (last year). We estimate $100 for food and transportation Kamloops/Bowron/Kamloops, for a total cost of $200-$250. (All prices in US$). Since we MUST make our reservations on March 1, we need to know of interest as soon as possible. For more information, call or e-mail Bruce McAlister at 425-821-6862, 70675.1764@compuserve.com.

Globe Trotting Orienteer Lives

While many of us might recall that COC/Husky’s Eric Bone represented USOF last summer in international competition, it may not be as common knowledge that COC/SAMM’s Bruce McAlister did the same at the International Orienteering Federation (IOF) meetings which took place along with the World Championships. Bruce is one of five on the IOF Technical Development Committee that researches, evaluates, and recommends technical equipment for orienteering [for example, the principal product of interest at the moment is the electronic punch.] He filled in for USOF President Gary Kraght at the IOF Council’s President’s session, as well as attending meetings of the IOF Mapping Committee at Grimstad. In the last year and a half, Bruce has attended IOF meetings in Israel, Norway and Denmark. And if you think that Bruce might still be a little low on airline frequent flyer miles, don’t worry as Bruce is now getting set to join the US Ski Orienteering Team in Austria this January where, besides helping out with ski team logistics, he will represent the Technical Development Committee at the meetings of the IOF Event Procedures Committee [yes, the subject will be electronic punching standards]. And then there is the IOF Congress in Portugal in July. And yes, Bruce pays his own way; he receives no subsidy from either USOF or IOF, but those senior airline coupons help a lot.

COC Annual Meeting Highlights

Congratulations to Bill Cusworth and Don Denbo, who were recognized with the Board of Director’s and President’s 1997 Service Award, respectively.

Congratulations and thank-you’s-in-advance to the following five COC elected Board of Directors: Bill Cusworth, Don Denbo, Peter Golde, Debbie Newell, and Jim Siscel. (If you are interested in serving in a COC leadership role this year, please contact one of these board members or attend the January meeting, which is describe in another article in the issue.)

Rick Breseman had a high-tech, and very well received, video show of the upcoming 1998 US Championships, that COC is hosting this August.

WIOL Newsletter On-line

This year anyone with access to the web can catch up on the WIOL newsletter by looking at in on-line. The site is being generously hosted by Howard Halter at: http://www.eskimo.com/~halter/orienteering/wiol/wiol.html. In addition to the results and standings being updated after every meet, we also feature color photos and selected route choice review, plus a puzzle or two. So visit us. (If you don't have access to the web and are a WIOL participant, you will receive 2 mid-season "snap-shots" of the webpage during the season. Additionally, all participants will receive a postcard prior to the WIOL Championships, with their start time and their standing, and the March/April issue of the Bearing 315, with a special WIOL wrap-up included.)

COC seeks "Radio Broadcaster"

COC is looking for someone with a great voice that is interested in being heard by millions (well, maybe less) each week. If you are interested in being the voice of the orienteering hotline and are willing to do the weekly updates, we are interested in having you "audition". (FYI—the audition is not too demanding and no prior broadcast or hotline experience is necessary, just a desire to do updates regularly.) For "auditions", please call Rick at 206-367-2606.

Two First and Two Second from One Family

The Breseman clan is piling up the points in WIOL and Winter O' Series competition. Elementary school orienteers Ryan and Kelsey Breseman are in first place in their WIOL group, while their parents are in second place in the adult Winter O' Series. So, if you want some pointers, you might want to ask the kids. To really get on their good side, you might offer Ryan a book to read, while Kelsey is partial to chocolate.

Tips & Techniques

[Image: Graphic of a dark triangle in a square box]

Move up! A column for the A.O.

(reprinted from the NEOC TIMES)
by Peter Amram

The desire in an A.O. (Average Orienteer) to move up—to compete on a higher course, up to yellow, up to orange, and beyond!—is primal. Sometimes you'd do just about anything to move up. But, my fellow A.O., would you ever, uh, follow someone in the woods? Huh?

The rules are clear. No giving or receiving help, and no following other runners. But practice is more ambiguous. At a local event you are helplessly lost and somebody competent-looking shows up? Of course you ask for help! And you had darn well better be prepared to give it too, when you're the one they ask which trail this is. Everyone has the right to enjoyment in orienteering.

(A-events are a different matter. I myself acknowledge occasionally consulting a passerby if I'm way overdue in finding one of those pesky orange-and-white triangles. Twice at West Point this former Marine PFC has had the pleasantly avuncular duty of pointing the way for a USMA cadet. Still, outright helping in the woods, though relatively common practice, is illegal. No doubt about it.)

But what about following other runners? Is there any shame in hitchhiking a ride now or then? Who will know? People do it. In the Blue Hills Traverse [a very long annual event in the New England area—Ed], following is actually encouraged. Some fold enter as virtual teams. In the Traverse, it is not unusual for a skirmish line or orienteers to search a ridge communally, like gleaners in a field.

But that's the Traverse, a special event. (Incidentally, the 25th running of the BHT is this November, and I intend to chivvy you—yes, you, an A.O.—into entering this year. More in future columns.)

As it turns out, besides a regard for O-ethics, there is an equally compelling if mundane reason not to follow in the woods. To wit: following is bad tactics. Following when you're lost will usually get you even loster. And that's not only because the person you are following may be lost too!

Let us examine some real-life dramas played out every weekend in New England. Only the names have been suppressed to protect my anonymity.

Scene 1: The control has got to be around here somewhere. Look, here comes another orienteer. He is heading purposefully toward the reentrant. Follow him! Yes! There's a cheerful bright control flag, at the boulder over there. Hi there, little friend. You were right here all along. Let's punch in. Oh, and check the code on this tag first. It's ZQ. ZQ? I'm looking for... GK. On a knoll, actually, now that I think of it. Damn!

Moral: When looking for Yellow #5, it avails one little to discover Orange #7 by means fair or foul.

Scene 2: Lost. Everything looks the same. But hark! Another orienteer. She is moving along as if she knows what she's doing. Let's follow. Look subtle. Lope along behind. I can keep up. Heh, heh. Soon we'll be there.

Reality: Madame is a red runner. You, however, are on orange today. Some 600 meters later, scene 1 will recur. You are deep in Nowheresville. A chill rain starts to fall. And it is growing dark.

Scene 3: Where am I? Where is the cursed control? Wait, help is at hand. I know this guy coming by. I know he's on my course. I saw him mark orange on his punch card. He started behind me and has thoughtfully caught up to help me. All I want is a quick tow to #5. I can do the rest myself. After him!

But: The International Orienteering Federation (IOF) has set the strict standard that anyone actually on your course whom you attempt to follow must be at least 29% faster than you in the 10K. Within 37 seconds you will lose sight of Sir Speedy. And you weren't checking your map for those 37 seconds, were you? Hmm?

All of which suggest that you learn: to keep constant close contact with the map; to relocate immediately to a strong linear feature when in doubt; and to trust no one but yourself to get you where you want to go.

Which is not only morally uplifting—it's really the fun of it all.

Horn Rapids

November 15, 1997
West Richland, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers

Meet Director & Course Setter: Michel Billaux
Control Pickup: Michel Billaux, Ivar Husa, Frank Skorina

Michel served up an enjoyable and challenging score-o course with a high control density near the start/finish, with decreasing density towards the outer bowels of the map. The area is very open with few trees to restrict visibility. This does not make the orienteering easy, on the contrary, there are fewer cues to orient yourself through the contours and into the controls.

Maximum fun must have been achieved as all participants were over the one hour "time limit" as they searched for "just a few more controls." We thank Todd Brown for bringing out a Benton City crew and once again Kim Oakes who brought another newcomer after bringing eight last meet.

This was our last scheduled meet of the year. We will be having an annual meeting sometime in December. Watch for it at an email box near you.

For this meet 22 controls were scattered about and participants found as many as they wanted. Points were calculated by awarding 1000 points for every control found, and subtracting 5 points for every second over 60 minutes.

December 13, 1997
Bellevue, WA
Sammamish Orienteering Club

Early morning frost was evident in this Bellvue park, but clear skies prevailed and temperatures warmed as the morning wore on. The first Washington Interscholastic "0" League meet-of-record for the season brought a large turnout, with groups from distant points. Bremerton, Oak Harbor, Bellingham, and Marysville were all represented.

The young people arrived all at aonce, but the fine work of Bill Cusworth and Don Denbo had them all (over 90) registered by 10:15 am. The start crew had everyone efficiently sent on their way with minimum delay.

Course designer Mark Howlett set up public courses 1, 2 and 4, with the longest including a map exchange up on the hillside on the east end of the park. WIOL participants were on courses, 3, 2, and 1, respectively, by age class. After most WIOL starters were out, course 3 was opened to the public.

It was clear at this meet that WIOL participation has grown enormously. The bare-bones minimum staff, for meets during league season requires 3 on starts, 3 on registreation, and 3 or 4 on finishes to keep everything running smoothly. It was a little tense at first when most WIOL runners were there ahead of the volunteer meet staffers.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

We are hoping for a ski-o meet this year. Bruce McAlister and Eric Bone revised the Lake Wenatchee map this past summer. 1998 Nike World Masters Games Orienteering Event
So, what are you doing next summer?

The countdown continues as eight months remain until the largest multisport competition in the world, the 1998 NIKE World Masters Games. Portland, Oregon, and several surrounding communities are gearing up to welcome 25,000 athletes from over 100 countries. 400 of these athletes will descend on Portland, OR and Goldendale, WA to compete in Orienteering.

The World Masters Games are looking for orienteers to volunteer their time and vast knowledge to help the Orienteering Sports Committee plan their events. Besides gaining invaluable event organizing experience and participating in a once in a lifetime event, volunteers will walk away with lots of cool stuff.

All Orienteering Committee Volunteers will be outfitted in limited edition NIKE polo shirts, caps and waist packs and receive coupons and discounts at many area merchants. At the conclusion of the Games, volunteers will receive an Official Volunteer Certificate and regal treatment at the Volunteer Appreciation Party. Perhaps the best gift is knowing that you had a significant role in promoting and producing a world-class international Orienteering Meet in front of a world wide audience. ESPN and ABC Sports are covering the Games and will feature Orienteering on one of their segments.

So what exactly is going on? Challenging O-action will take place in Portland and Goldendale, WA, from August 10-14, 1998. There will be a Training Event as well as a Short Course and Classic 1 and Classic 2 meets. Medals will be awarded for both A and B levels in each event.

With the help of an organized and enthusiastic Orienteering community, the event will be an incredible success. For more information on positions available on the Orienteering Sports Committee, please contact Gorden Bowen, Orienteering Sport Volunteer Director, at (503) 235-6876, or by email at gordonb@europa.com. To receive a volunteer application form or more information on the 1998 NIKE World Masters Games please call (503) 226-1998, or visit the on-line Web site at http://www.worldmasters.org.

There is a possibility of a canoe trip on the Bowron Lake Circuit the week between the Canadian Champs and the North American Champs at Kamloops (July 26-August 1). This approximately 120 km canoe trip in the heart of the Canadian Rockies with glimpses of Mt Robson is one of the premier canoe trips in the world. We need a group of 7 to 12. The trip is not demanding physically, but does require prior knowledge of canoeing. Permit cost is $45 per canoe (2 persons), canoe rental (2 persons) at Bowron Lake is about $175 (last year). We estimate $100 for food and transportation Kamloops/Bowron/Kamloops, for a total cost of $200-$250. (All prices in US$). While many of us might recall that COC/Husky’s Eric Bone represented USOF last summer in international competition, it may not be as common knowledge that COC/SAMM’s Bruce McAlister did the same at the International Orienteering Federation (IOF) meetings which took place along with the World Championships. Bruce is one of five on the IOF Technical Development Committee that researches, evaluates, and recommends technical equipment for orienteering [for example, the principal product of interest at the moment is the electronic punch.] He filled in for USOF President Gary Kraght at the IOF Council’s President’s session, as well as attending meetings of the IOF Mapping Committee at Grimstad. In the last year and a half, Bruce has attended IOF meetings in Israel, Norway and Denmark. And if you think that Bruce might still be a little low on airline frequent flyer miles, don’t worry as Bruce is now getting set to join the US Ski Orienteering Team in Austria this January where, besides helping out with ski team logistics, he will represent the Technical Development Committee at the meetings of the IOF Event Procedures Committee [yes, the subject will be electronic punching standards]. And then there is the IOF Congress in Portugal in July. And yes, Bruce pays his own way; he receives no subsidy from either USOF or IOF, but those senior airline coupons help a lot.

Move up! A column for the A.O.
(reprinted from the NEOC TIMES) by Peter Amram

The desire in an A.O. (Average Orienteer) to move up—to compete on a higher course, up to yellow, up to orange, and beyond!—is primal. Sometimes you'd do just about anything to move up. But, my fellow A.O., would you ever, uh, follow someone in the woods? Huh?

The rules are clear. No giving or receiving help, and no following other runners. But practice is more ambiguous. At a local event you are helplessly lost and somebody competent-looking shows up? Of course you ask for help! And you had darn well better be prepared to give it too, when you're the one they ask which trail this is. Everyone has the right to enjoyment in orienteering.

(A-events are a different matter. I myself acknowledge occasionally consulting a passerby if I'm way overdue in finding one of those pesky orange-and-white triangles. Twice at West Point this former Marine PFC has had the pleasantly avuncular duty of pointing the way for a USMA cadet. Still, outright helping in the woods, though relatively common practice, is illegal. No doubt about it.)

But what about following other runners? Is there any shame in hitchhiking a ride now or then? Who will know? People do it. In the Blue Hills Traverse [a very long annual event in the New England area—Ed], following is actually encouraged. Some fold enter as virtual teams. In the Traverse, it is not unusual for a skirmish line or orienteers to search a ridge communally, like gleaners in a field.
But that's the Traverse, a special event. (Incidentally, the 25th running of the BHT is this November, and I intend to chivvy you—yes, you, an A.O.—into entering this year. More in future columns.)

As it turns out, besides a regard for O-ethics, there is an equally compelling if mundane reason not to follow in the woods. To wit: following is bad tactics. Following when you're lost will usually get you even loster. And that's not only because the person you are following may be lost too!
Let us examine some real-life dramas played out every weekend in New England. Only the names have been suppressed to protect my anonymity.

Scene 1: The control has got to be around here somewhere. Look, here comes another orienteer. He is heading purposefully toward the reentrant. Follow him! Yes! There's a cheerful bright control flag, at the boulder over there. Hi there, little friend. You were right here all along. Let's punch in. Oh, and check the code on this tag first. It's ZQ. ZQ? I'm looking for... GK. On a knoll, actually, now that I think of it. Damn!
Moral: When looking for Yellow #5, it avails one little to discover Orange #7 by means fair or foul.

Scene 2: Lost. Everything looks the same. But hark! Another orienteer. She is moving along as if she knows what she's doing. Let's follow. Look subtle. Lope along behind. I can keep up. Heh, heh. Soon we'll be there.
Reality: Madame is a red runner. You, however, are on orange today. Some 600 meters later, scene 1 will recur. You are deep in Nowheresville. A chill rain starts to fall. And it is growing dark.

Scene 3: Where am I? Where is the cursed control? Wait, help is at hand. I know this guy coming by. I know he's on my course. I saw him mark orange on his punch card. He started behind me and has thoughtfully caught up to help me. All I want is a quick tow to #5. I can do the rest myself. After him!

But: The International Orienteering Federation (IOF) has set the strict standard that anyone actually on your course whom you attempt to follow must be at least 29% faster than you in the 10K. Within 37 seconds you will lose sight of Sir Speedy. And you weren't checking your map for those 37 seconds, were you? Hmm?

All of which suggest that you learn: to keep constant close contact with the map; to relocate immediately to a strong linear feature when in doubt; and to trust no one but yourself to get you where you want to go.
Which is not only morally uplifting—it's really the fun of it all.

December 13, 1997 Bellevue, WA Sammamish Orienteering Club

Early morning frost was evident in this Bellvue park, but clear skies prevailed and temperatures warmed as the morning wore on. The first Washington Interscholastic "0" League meet-of-record for the season brought a large turnout, with groups from distant points. Bremerton, Oak Harbor, Bellingham, and Marysville were all represented.

The young people arrived all at aonce, but the fine work of Bill Cusworth and Don Denbo had them all (over 90) registered by 10:15 am. The start crew had everyone efficiently sent on their way with minimum delay.

Course designer Mark Howlett set up public courses 1, 2 and 4, with the longest including a map exchange up on the hillside on the east end of the park. WIOL participants were on courses, 3, 2, and 1, respectively, by age class. After most WIOL starters were out, course 3 was opened to the public.

It was clear at this meet that WIOL participation has grown enormously. The bare-bones minimum staff, for meets during league season requires 3 on starts, 3 on registreation, and 3 or 4 on finishes to keep everything running smoothly. It was a little tense at first when most WIOL runners were there ahead of the volunteer meet staffers.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.

As a U.S. Citizen or U.S. Resident, I hereby apply for USOF membership. I understand that I am entitled to a one-year subscription to Orienteering North America, voting rights, and membership in the IOF. Please print.
Member of a chartered club? Club____________________

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
